text: It's been historically cyclical because DRAM memory technologies only change every 5-10 years and they were historically a 'good enough' part of a stack, whereas cpu/gpu are normally the bottlenecks that see new product cycles every 1-2 years, and node changes every 2-3x. So memory manufacturing would mature and demand would taper for top end and it becomes a race to the bottom on pricing. We're still on DDR5 from 2020, for instance. With DDR4 still going strong up until recently (released in 2014). The difference now (with large models with large datasets) is that memory is the real bottleneck (each subsequent model needs exponentially more data), and what was once a niche product (HBM) is now the only viable thing for scale. We're at the beginning of a new memory cycle, and HBMs challenges get harder the more you densify it. The maturity cycle will be much longer, if at all ever reaching 90% yields.
label: analysis

text: It is possible that in 2028 we may see a glut of memory supply, and these stocks may come down or be stagnant for next 3-4 years, but you might have made your profit. Or the downturns might not be severe.

text: Bubbles can work oddly. If enough people think you are in a bubble the burst may never happen because they typically trade thinking it will go down relieving the downward pressure. They tend to burst when you don't think you are in a bubble. The last big one happened with very few investors knew it was coming. Today we have social media where everyone knows everything so no telling if we will get a burst like before. Someone who predicts when if it does happen would get rich fast if they backup their guess.
